summary_opm:
📄 TECHNICAL AND MANAGERIAL EVALUATION COMMENT FOR JULIUS BUTARDO (Project Superintendent)

The evaluation indicates that the Project Superintendent is still progressing in the development of his technical and managerial competencies. Based solely on the Technical & Managerial Evaluation, he achieved an overall score of 34.22%, which reflects performance within the Project Superintendent 2 Threshold. This score is based on consistent monthly assessments across key functional areas over the past evaluation period.

This score is derived from observations and site inspections conducted over the past three months. The following areas were identified as needing improvement during the execution of the project under his supervision:

1. Strengthen his oversight in ensuring that work packages, drawings, and method statements are fully understood by the crews and subcontractors before execution.

2. Needs improvement in enforcement of documentation requirements.

3. Improve the timely submission and follow-up of material requests.

4. Improve the translation of schedules into realistic, achievable look‑ahead plans.

5. Improve manpower allocation, monitoring, and performance assessment to ensure alignment with look‑ahead schedules.

6. Strengthen compliance with safety protocols and 5S implementation.

7. Strengthen monitoring and reporting practices for accurate progress visibility and enable timely interventions to keep activities on track.


Despite the areas needing improvement, the Project Superintendent demonstrated several positive qualities that support his continued growth:

1. Demonstrates reliable knowledge on electrical, fdas and fire protection works.

2. Demonstrates greater maturity in handling project.

3. Demonstrates willingness to support the company’s growth, adapt to organizational changes, and align with established goals.

4. Shows a clear understanding of the need to maximize profit and maintain efficient work‑cost practices.


Overall, the Project Superintendent shows potential for continued growth as he further develops his technical and managerial capabilities. While his current performance reflects areas that require improvement—particularly in planning, coordination, documentation, and compliance—his demonstrated strengths, such as discipline‑specific knowledge, growing maturity, willingness to support organizational goals, and awareness of cost efficiency, indicate a positive trajectory. With focused guidance and consistent application of the identified improvement areas, he is expected to enhance his effectiveness and contribute more strongly to project delivery and company objectives
summary_qaqc:
📄 QAQC EVALUATION COMMENT FOR JULIUS BUTARDO (Project Superintendent)

Julius Butardo’s QAQC evaluation for the period reflects a developing level of performance in quality governance, documentation oversight, planning, and field decision‑making. Based on the consolidated scores across all QAQC functional areas, his overall rating falls within the Project Superintendent 1 threshold (25.1%–33%). This indicates that the foundational competencies required for the role are present, but there is still significant room for improvement to reach the higher superintendent levels.

It is important to emphasize that this QAQC scoring system is newly implemented, and as expected, most personnel—including Julius—are still adjusting to the more structured, metrics‑driven evaluation framework. This transition naturally results in lower initial scores, as the system now captures performance gaps more accurately, especially in areas related to documentation completeness, inspection readiness, and compliance with QAQC protocols.

His QAQC performance was also directly affected by the Non‑Conformance (NC) records logged on his site. The uploaded file shows multiple NC entries under the Demerits Log, including misalignment of waterlines, incomplete waterproofing checklists, and grout quality issues, all marked as “Responsible – 2” . These NCs fall under his scope of responsibility as Project Superintendent and therefore lowered his QAQC monthly averages.

Despite these challenges, Julius has shown consistency in routine inspections, coordination with QAQC personnel, and timely submission of inspection requests. His month‑to‑month scores demonstrate stable performance, suggesting that the baseline skills are present and can be strengthened with more deliberate QAQC leadership and preventive site management.

📌 Recommendations for Improvement

1. Strengthen Preventive QAQC Controls

  • Conduct pre‑work briefings to ensure crews understand ITPs, method statements, and quality checkpoints.

  • Increase supervision during critical activities to prevent workmanship issues that lead to NCRs.

2. Improve NCR Management and Closure

  • Prioritize immediate corrective actions and root‑cause analysis.

  • Implement a weekly NCR review with site engineers and foremen.

  • Track closure timelines more aggressively to avoid backlog accumulation.

3. Enhance Documentation and Inspection Readiness

  • Ensure IRs are complete, accurate, and submitted ahead of schedule.

  • Regularly audit test reports, QAQC logs, and material approvals for completeness and traceability.

4. Strengthen Coordination With QAQC Teams

  • Engage QAQC personnel earlier during planning and sequencing.

  • Conduct joint inspections more frequently to reduce rework and improve compliance.

5. Elevate Field Decision‑Making and Accountability

  • Address recurring workmanship issues through coaching and corrective toolbox meetings.

  • Demonstrate stronger ownership of site quality outcomes by anticipating risks before they escalate.

📌 Recommendations for Improvement Based on Demerits

1. Strengthen Workmanship Supervision

  • Increase presence during critical installations (plumbing, waterproofing, ceiling works, finishing).

  • Conduct pre‑work briefings to ensure crews understand specifications and quality checkpoints.

2. Improve Documentation Compliance

  • Enforce strict completion of checklists, especially for waterproofing and other hold‑point activities.

  • Conduct random audits of forms, logs, and inspection records.

3. Enhance Preventive Quality Controls

  • Require mock‑ups or samples (e.g., faucet reference for waterline alignment) before full installation.

  • Implement a “first‑unit inspection” approach to catch errors early.

4. Strengthen Coordination With QAQC

  • Engage QAQC personnel before starting activities prone to deviations.

  • Conduct joint inspections to ensure compliance and reduce rework.

5. Address Recurring Issues Through Coaching

  • Discuss repeated deviations with foremen and workers to prevent recurrence.

  • Use toolbox meetings to reinforce quality expectations and corrective actions.
summary_qs:
📄 QS EVALUATION COMMENT FOR JULIUS BUTARDO (Project Superintendent)

Julius Butardo’s QS evaluation for the period reflects a developing level of competency in measurement accuracy, cost estimation, and cost control. Based on the consolidated scores across the QS functional areas, his overall rating falls within the Project Superintendent 1 threshold (25.1%–33%). This indicates that while the essential QS responsibilities are being carried out, there is still considerable room for improvement to reach the higher superintendent competency levels.

It is important to emphasize that this QS scoring system is newly implemented, and as expected, most personnel—including Julius—are still adjusting to the more structured, metrics‑driven evaluation framework. This transition naturally results in lower initial scores, as the system now captures performance gaps more clearly, especially in areas related to documentation accuracy, cost monitoring, and early detection of quantity discrepancies.

Another significant factor affecting Julius’s QS rating is the presence of non‑conformance records (NCRs) and punch‑list findings documented on his assigned site. These deviations directly influence QS performance because non‑conformances often lead to rework, quantity adjustments, additional material usage, and cost variances. As the Project Superintendent, Julius holds operational accountability for these deviations, and their recurrence impacts both cost control and measurement accuracy.

Despite these challenges, Julius has shown consistency in performing quantity take‑offs and maintaining basic cost‑tracking routines. His month‑to‑month scores demonstrate stable performance, indicating that the foundational QS skills are present and can be strengthened with more deliberate oversight and coordination.

📌 Recommendations for Improvement

1. Strengthen Measurement Accuracy and Verification

  • Conduct joint measurements with the QS team to minimize discrepancies.

  • Ensure drawings, revisions, and site conditions are aligned before measurement.

  • Reduce rework by enforcing strict adherence to approved plans and specifications.

2. Improve Cost Control and Budget Monitoring

  • Track material usage more closely to prevent wastage and cost overruns.

  • Validate quantities before procurement to avoid excess ordering.

  • Review weekly cost reports to identify trends and potential overruns early.

3. Enhance Documentation and Reporting Discipline

  • Ensure measurement sheets, logs, and quantity reports are complete, accurate, and timely.

  • Maintain updated records of variations, site instructions, and material consumption.

4. Strengthen Coordination With QS and QAQC Teams

  • Align QS activities with QAQC findings to prevent cost impacts from NCRs.

  • Participate in NCR root‑cause analysis to reduce quantity adjustments caused by rework.

5. Improve Preventive Site Controls

  • Conduct toolbox meetings to address recurring workmanship issues that lead to cost deviations.

  • Enforce stricter compliance with method statements and approved drawings to avoid unnecessary material usage.

6. Strengthen Labor Cost Analysis Skills

  • Study actual manpower outputs and productivity rates from the site

  • Compare estimated labor costs with actual incurred costs to identify gaps

  • Develop more detailed breakdowns of labor activities and resource allocation

  • Coordinate closely with site supervisors to validate assumptions
